---
title: Ex.1 Parsing Protein Structures
format: html
Authors: "Marta Aransay, Carlota de la Concepción, Marta Rodrigo, Lucía Santos"
date: 04/03/2026
---

# Introduction

Structural bioinformatics integrates computational methods, data resources and algorithms to study, analyze and predict the three-dimensional organization of biomacromolecules. High-resolution structural information  provides insights into biological function and enables the characterization of molecular effects of mutations, ligand binding and intermolecular interactions.

Proteins are macromolecules whose function is highly related to their three-dimensional structure. Their organization can be described in four levels, which depend on folding. Protein folding is driven by non-covalent interactions between atoms of residues of the same molecule, intramolecular, or other molecules, intermolecular. These interactions include hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions and electrostatic forces. Structural bioinformatics aims to characterize these interactions through computational analysis of experimentally determined or predicted structures.

The main repository of experimentally determined macromolecuar structures is the Protein Data Bank (PDB), which stores three-dimensional structures obtained by X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and cryo-electron microscopy. PDB files contain detailed metadata, experimental information, protein sequences, and three-dimensional atomic coordinates. Through computational analysis, this information can be extracted from these files.

To analyze protein architecture contact maps are commonly used as a simplified representation of the three-dimensional structure. These maps use pairwise distance matrices to provide a two-dimensional visualization of the interactions between the C$\alpha$  of all the residues in a protein. A distance threshold is established to determine if two residues interact or not. Specific patterns are associated with specific secondary structure elements, so it is possible to infer the spatial organization of proteins.

Nowadays, although modelling and structure prediction methods have achieved high levels of accuracy, the analysis of atomic coordinates is still essential to understand the spatial organization of proteins and macromolecular complexes.


## Objective

The main objective of this project is to implement computational functions to:

1. Parse PDB files to extract structural metadata and atomic coordinates.
2. Compute pairwise distance matrices between C$\alpha$ atoms of protein residues.
3. Plot contact maps to characterize the structural organization and oligomeric state of the analyzed proteins.

---

# Methods

## Data Collection

RCSB-PDB (Research Collaboratory for Structural Bioinformatics Protein Data Bank, https://www.rcsb.org/) is a repository of experimentally determined, and also computationally predicted, structures of proteins, nucleic acids and macromolecular complexes. 

Each protein in this database is identified with a unique four-character alphanumeric identifier. This identifier allows access to detailed information about the protein, such as atomic coordinates, sequence, experimental methodology and associated metadata.

The structures analyzed in this project correspond to the following proteins:

- **Plasma Retinol-Binding Protein Precursor (*Homo sapiens*, PDB ID: *1RBP*)**, which is involved in retinol transport in humans.
- **Carbonic Anhydrase (*Methanosarcina thermophila*, PDB ID: *1QRE*)**, which regulates pH by catalyzing the reaction between $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ to produce bicarbonate and protons.
- **Catalase (*Homo sapiens*, PDB ID: *1DGF*)**, which cells from oxidative damage by decomposing $H_2O_2$ into water and oxygen.

## Parsing PDB Files

::: {.callout-note}
 Before introducing the main functions of the project, it is important to highlight the implmentation of the @setup-libraries function, which initializes all required libraries and dependencies, avoiding redundancy and improving code readability.  
:::


The data base PDB is one of the most widely used worldwide in the field of bioinformatics, among other aspects for its accessibility and the large amount of information it contains. Taking advantage of the possibility of querying through its API, we have been able to extract the necessary data through a parsing function @get_metadata_function.

The objective of this function is to parse the PDB files of the studied proteins, extracting the relevant information for subsequent analysis. Specifically, we have obtained the descriptive and experimental information associated with each entry of the Protein Data Bank.

The function defined as @get_metadata_function performs two independent queries to the RCSB PDB REST API (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html), one at the structural level (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html#tag/Entry-Service/operation/getEntryById) and another at the polymer entity level (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html#tag/Entity-Service/operation/getPolymerEntityById). This separation allows us to access both the experimental and general details of the protein and the specific information related to it. 

In first place, the function implements a basic system of error handling to ensure that the queries are successful. This is approached through the definition of the function @handle_API_error_function, which checks the status code of the response and returns an appropriate message in case of an error. 

Once the queries are successful, the function extracts the relevant information from the JSON responses:

```{python}
#| echo: false

import pandas as pd

table_info = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metadata": ["PDB ID", "Protein Name", "Source Organism", "Determination Method", "Authors", "Release Date", "Resolution", "Number of Peptide Chains", "Number of Other Molecules"],
    "Description": ["Unique identifier for the protein structure", "The name of the protein", "The organism from which the protein was derived", "The method used to determine the protein structure (e.g., X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy)", "The researchers who contributed to the determination of the structure", "The release date of the structure", "The resolution of the structure, if applicable", "The number of peptide chains present in the structure", "The number of other molecules (e.g., ligands, water) present in the structure"],
    "Source (API JSON field)": ["rcsb_id", "rcsb_polymer_entity.pdbx_description", "rcsb_entity_source_organism", "exptl.method", "rcsb_authors", "rcsb_accession_info.initial_release_date", "rcsb_entry_info.resolution_combined", "rcsb_entry_info.polymer_entity_count_protein", "rcsb_entry_info.nonpolymer_entity_count"]
})

table_info

```

There are also some fields with multiple values, such as authors, experimental methods, etc. For those cases, an auxiliary function @extract_metadata_function is used to concatenate the elements of the list into a single string, separating the values with commas. This facilitates the presentation of the information and its subsequent analysis.

All this information is organized into a structured fromat, as it is a dictionary, which allows its conversion into a pandas DataFrame and its export to a CSV file for further analysis and visualization. This is done through the @metadata_to_csv_function, which takes the metadata dictionary and the desired file name as input and saves the DataFrame as a CSV file. 

### Code Implementation

```{python}
#| label: setup-libraries
#| message: false
#| warning: false

import requests
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches 

```

```{python}
#| label: handle_API_error_function

def handle_API_error(id, status_code = None, flag_timeout = False):
    """
    Handles HTTP request errors or timeouts and prints informative messages in
    order to centralize the management of API request errors.

    Parameters
    ----------
    id : str
        PDB ID queried in the request.
    status_code : int
        HTTP status code returned by the request. 
        None by default (in case of timeout error).
    flag_timeout : bool
        Indicates whether the request failed due to a timeout.
        If True, the status_code is ignored.
        alse by default.

    Returns
    -------
        bool (True)
            True if the request was successful (status code 200).
            None returned for any error or timeout condition.
    """
    if flag_timeout: # If a timeout occurred, skip all other checks
        print(f"[408] Timeout error for ID: {id}.")
        
    else:
        if status_code == 200: # If the request was successful, returns True
            return True
        
        elif status_code == 301 or status_code == 302:
            # Resource has been permanently or temporarily moved
            print(f"[301] or [302] The object was moved to other route")

        elif status_code == 400:
            # Request processed but considered unsuccessful
            print(f"[400] Not valid ID: {id}")

        elif status_code == 404:
            # Request processed but no result was obtained
            print(f"[404] Result not found for ID: {id}")

        elif status_code == 429:
            # Request rejected due to request rate limits
            print(f"[429] Error: Too many requests.")

        elif status_code == 500 or status_code == 503:
            # Request failed due to internal server issues
            print("[500] or [503] Internal Server Error")

        else: # For any other error status code
            print(f"Error: {status_code}")
        
        return # For all cases except status_code == 200, function returns None
```

```{python}
#| label: extract_metadata_function
def extract_join_metadata(data_dict, metadata_key, subkey=None):
    """
    Extract values from a metadata dictionary and join them into a string.

    Parameters
    ----------
    data_dict : dict
        Dictionary containing the metadata entries.
    metadata_key : str
        Key in `data_dict` whose value should be a list.
    subkey : str, optional
        Key to use within each list element.  
        If `None` the list elements are used directly.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        Comma-separated string of extracted values.
        "N/A"` if the list is empty or no values were found.
    """
    # Retrieve the list of items associated with metadata_key
    items = data_dict.get(metadata_key, []) # default to an empty list if not found

    extracted_items = [] # empty list

    if subkey: # In case there is a subkey
        for item in items: # Iterate through items
            # Get the value for the specific subkey
            value = item.get(subkey) # NO second arg to avoid list of "N/A"
            if value: 
                # If there is a value, it is added to the list
                extracted_items.append(value)
   
    # Return a string (comma-separated) if data was found 
    if extracted_items:
        return ", ".join(extracted_items) 
    else: # Otherwise, return "N/A"
        return "N/A" 
```

```{python}
#| label: get_metadata_function

def get_metadata_info(pdb_id):
    """
    Parse a PDB (Protein Data Bank) file and extract metadata information.
    Extracts and formats some metadata via extract_join_metadata function.
    Handles API errors via handle_API_error function.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    pdb_id : str
        Unique identifier for the PDB file.
    
    Returns
    -------
    metadata : dict
        Dictionary containing PDB metadata.
        Or None if an error occurs.
    """
    # Define endpoints for RCSB PDB Data API 
    # Entry API: Global metadata -> PDB ID; Determination Method; Authors; 
    #                               Release Date; Resolution; Number of peptide chains; 
    #                               Number of Other molecules
    url_entry = f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/entry/{pdb_id}"

    # Entity API: Specific data for cheminal entities
    entity_id = 1 # Using 1 for the primary polymer (protein)
    url_entity = f"https://data.rcsb.org/rest/v1/core/polymer_entity/{pdb_id}/{entity_id}" 

    # Handling with Timeout and Errors (only two possibilities: 200 / 404)
    try:
        response_entry = requests.get(url_entry,
                                     timeout = 100) 
        response_entity = requests.get(url_entity,
                                      timeout = 100) 

    except requests.exceptions.Timeout: # Handling Timeout Error
        handle_API_error(pdb_id, flag_timeout = True)
        return
    
    # Validate that PDB ID exists in both endopoints
    flag_entry = handle_API_error(pdb_id, status_code = response_entry.status_code)
    flag_entity = handle_API_error(pdb_id, status_code = response_entity.status_code)

    if not flag_entry or not flag_entity:
        return
    
    # Execution continues only if a 200 OK status is received
    
    # Parse JSON responses
    data_entry = response_entry.json()
    data_entity = response_entity.json()

    ############ Metadata extraction ############

    # ---- PDB ID
    id = data_entry.get("rcsb_id", "N/A") # Default to "N/A" if rcsb_id is not found

    # ---- Protein name
    # Default to empty dict to safely chain subsequent .get() calls
    polymer_entity = data_entity.get("rcsb_polymer_entity", {}) 
    prot_name = polymer_entity.get("pdbx_description", "N/A") 
    
    # ---- Source Organism
    # Extract and format via extract_join_metadata
    source_organisms = extract_join_metadata(data_entity,
                                            "rcsb_entity_source_organism", 
                                            subkey="ncbi_scientific_name") 

    # ---- Determination Method
    det_method = extract_join_metadata(data_entry, 
                                      "exptl", 
                                      subkey="method")

    # ---- Authors
    primary_cit = data_entry.get("rcsb_primary_citation", {}) 
    authors = extract_join_metadata(data_entry, 
                                   "rcsb_authors") 

    # ---- Release date
    acc_info = data_entry.get("rcsb_accession_info", {}) 
    date = acc_info.get("initial_release_date", "N/A")

    # ---- Resolution
    entry_info = data_entry.get("rcsb_entry_info", {}) 
    resolution = entry_info.get("resolution_combined", []) 
    if resolution: # If resolution is not an empty list
        resolution = resolution[0] # To remove "[]" 
    else:
        resolution = "N/A"

    # ---- Number of peptide chains
    num_chains = entry_info.get("polymer_entity_count_protein", "N/A")

    # ---- Other molecules
    molecules = entry_info.get("nonpolymer_entity_count", "N/A")

    ############ Metadata dictionary ############

    # Assemble the metadata dictionary
    metadata = {
        "PDB ID": id,
        "Protein Name": prot_name,
        "Source Organims": source_organisms,
        "Determination Method": det_method,
        "Authors": authors,
        "Release Date": date,
        "Resolution": resolution,
        "Number of Peptide Chains": num_chains,
        "Number of Other Molecules": molecules
    }
    return metadata
```

```{python}
#| label: metadata_to_csv_function

def save_metadata_csv(pdb_id, filename):
    """
    Extracts metadata from PDB ID via get_metadata_info function
    Stores the results in a pandas DataFrame and writes it to a CSV file.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    pdb_id : str
        PDB ID to be parsed.
    filename : str
        The output CSV file path where metadata will be saved.
    
    Returns
    -------
    df : pd.DataFrame
        A pandas DataFrame containing the metadata.
    """
    # Fetch metadata dictionary for the single PDB ID
    metadata_info = get_metadata_info(pdb_id) 

    # Pass as a list to avoid "Scalar values" index error in Pandas
    df = pd.DataFrame([metadata_info])

    # Export the DataFrame to a CSV file
    df.to_csv(filename, index=False) # index=False to prevent writing row numbers
    
    print(f"Metadata for {pdb_id} successfully saved to {filename}")

    return df
```

## Structural Infrormation Extraction and Distance Matrix Calculation

To understand how a protein folds and how its redidues interact with each other, it is needed to extract the spatial coordinates of the aminoacids. Then it is possible to calculate the pairwise distances between them.

PDB stablished a text standard  and human readable format to store the 3D structures of biomolecules. Each line in a PDB file corresponds to a specific type of information, they are fixed register lines with specific fields. The file start with a header section, which contains general information about the structure, such as the PDB ID, protein name, realesed date, etc. Then, there is a section with the atomic coordinates, where each line corresponds to an atom in the structure and contains information about its type, residue, chain, and 3D coordinates (x, y, z). Those lines start with the keyword "ATOM". Finally, there is a section with the connectivity information, which describes how the atoms are connected to form residues and chains.

To extract the mentioned data, we have implemented the function @get_structural_info_function, which make a request to the RCSB PSB API to obtain textual data of the PDB file. Then, it parses the response to extract the atomic coordinates of the C$\alpha$ atoms, which are commonly used to represent the backbone of the protein structure. 

The function also, traduce the aminoacid three-letter code to one-letter code, through a mapping dictionary. 

Finally, the function extract the chain, number of residue and the cartesian coordinates (x, y, z), and return a dataframe that will be used for the subsequent distance and contact map calculations.

- FALTA AÑADIR FUNCIONES AUXILIARES QUE USE
- FALTA AÑADIR EL CÁLCULO DE LAS DISTANCAS 
- FALTA AÑADIR EL CÓDIGO


## Distance and Contact Map Calculations

[Primero hacer una introduccion a que es la distancia el mapa de contacto (por qué son útiles), meter alguna explicacion matemática, y luego lo mismo que antes, describir las partes más importantes del código.]

\[
d_{ij}^2 = (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 + (z_i - z_j)^2
\]

---

# Results

TABLA CON LOS RESULTADOS DE LAS DISTANCIAS Y CONTACTOS

---

# Discussion

---

# Conclusion

---

# Annexes

## Work Register

[Aquí básicamente pondría el paso a paso que hemos seguido en github, con los commits, etc. para que se vea el proceso de trabajo]

The development of this project has followed a workflow managed via GitHub, in order it can be reproduced.

The following table sumarizes the main stages of the project.

```{python}
#| echo: false
table_github = pd.DataFrame({
    "Phase": [
        "Setup", 
        "Metadata", 
        "Protein structural information", 
        "Distance matrix", 
        "Contact maps", 
        "Report writing"], 
    "Description":[
        "Repository initialization and project structuring", 
        "Access to each protein key information via APIs and store it in a dataframe", 
        "Get access to PDB files and parse them to gen atomic coordinates",
        "Compute the distances matrix between $C_{\\alpha}$ atoms",
        "Plot intra/interchain contacts giving a threshold",
        "Create the final file and include all the previous analysis"]})
table_github
```

Every modification is documented through a descriptive commit message.

**Repository Access**: https://github.com/MCML-Projects/STRBIO.git
